title: Why the return of self-trust is the foundation everything else builds on
author: Kalesh

You stopped trusting yourself for good reason 

 I remember the exact moment I realized I couldn't trust my own gut anymore. I was twenty-seven, sitting i...
htmlContent:
You stopped trusting yourself for good reason

I remember the exact moment I realized I couldn't trust my own gut anymore. I was twenty-seven, sitting in a therapist's office, and she asked me what I needed. I opened my mouth. Nothing came out. Not because I didn't have needs. I had plenty. But somewhere along the way, I learned that what I needed didn't matter. That my instincts were wrong. That my body's signals were overreactions. That trusting myself was dangerous.

Sound familiar? Most of us with complex trauma didn't wake up one day and decide to abandon self-trust. We were trained out of it. By parents who couldn't handle our emotions. By caregivers who punished our boundaries. By systems that demanded compliance over connection. By a world that told us our perception of reality was distorted.

Here's the thing: self-trust isn't something you lose because you're broken. It's something you lose because it kept you safe. If trusting your gut meant getting hurt, your nervous system did the math and decided that self-distrust was the safer bet. That's not a character flaw. That's survival intelligence. Wild, right?

But here's where it gets tricky. That survival strategy worked when you were small and powerless. It kept you alive. But now? Now it's the thing keeping you stuck. Because without self-trust, everything else wobbles. Every boundary. Every relationship. Every decision about your own life. You're building on sand.

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, talks about how our nervous system is constantly scanning for safety. And when you don't trust yourself, your system never fully settles. You're always waiting for the other shoe to drop. Always second-guessing. Always looking outside yourself for answers that only you can provide.

Sit with that for a second. What would it feel like to actually believe yourself?

The wellness industry lied to you about what comes first

Everywhere you look, there's another list of things you need to do to heal. Meditate. Journal. Do yoga. Set boundaries. Affirm yourself. Eat clean. Sleep eight hours. Drink more water. And sure, those things can help. But they're not the foundation. They can't be. Because none of them work if you don't trust yourself enough to do them consistently.

Think about it. How many times have you started a new morning routine, only to abandon it three days in? How many times have you told yourself you'd set a boundary, then folded the moment someone pushed back? How many times have you bought a journal, wrote in it for a week, then left it on the shelf collecting dust?

Something I want to name explicitly. If you're reading this and thinking "this doesn't apply to me because my trauma wasn't bad enough" - I hear you. And I want to challenge that thought gently. Trauma isn't measured by what happened to you. It's measured by what happened inside you. Two people can experience the same event and have completely different nervous system responses. Neither is wrong. Neither is "too sensitive." Your response was your response. It was real. It counts.

Gabor Mate says it perfectly: "Trauma is not what happens to you. It's what happens inside you as a result of what happened to you." So if your body carries the imprint of something - if your nervous system is dysregulated, if your relationships are affected, if your sense of self was shaped by it - then it matters. Regardless of how it looks from the outside. Regardless of whether anyone else would call it "trauma." Your body doesn't lie.

That's not laziness. That's not lack of discipline. That's your nervous system saying, "I don't trust that this will work. I don't trust that I can follow through. I don't trust that I deserve consistency." And honestly, your nervous system has data to back that up. Years of it. Decades, maybe.

Peter Levine, who created Somatic Experiencing, talks about how trauma lives in the body. Not in your thoughts. Not in your memories. In your actual physical tissue. And one of the core things trauma does is destroy your ability to trust your own interoceptive signals. Your hunger cues. Your fatigue levels. Your emotional responses. Your sense of what's safe and what's not. All scrambled.

So when someone tells you to "just trust the process" or "trust your journey," you might feel a flash of anger. Good. That anger is honest. Because you can't trust a process you've never experienced working. You can't trust a journey when you don't trust the person navigating it.

That person is you.

A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection is Radical Acceptance (paid link). Tara Brach's work is not about bypassing your distrust. It's about meeting it with enough compassion that you can start to loosen its grip.

Trust is built in small moments, not big declarations

Here's the part nobody tells you. Self-trust doesn't come from a dramatic breakthrough. It doesn't come from a weekend retreat or a single therapy session. It comes from a thousand tiny moments where you keep a promise to yourself.

You say you'll go to bed by eleven. You do it. You say you'll take a five minute break when you feel overwhelmed. You take it. You say you'll drink water when you're thirsty. You drink it. These sound stupidly simple. I know. But stay with me here.

Each time you keep a small promise, you're sending a signal to your nervous system. "See? I can be trusted. I follow through. I'm reliable." And your nervous system starts to recalibrate. Slowly. Painfully slowly. But it happens.

This is why I tell people to start smaller than they think they need to. If you can't trust yourself to exercise for thirty minutes, try five. If you can't trust yourself to meditate for ten minutes, try one. If you can't trust yourself to say no to a big request, try saying no to a small one. The size of the promise doesn't matter. What matters is that you keep it.

Notice what just happened in your body when I said that. Did your chest tighten? Did your mind start arguing? "That's not enough. That won't work. I need to do more." That's the voice of the trauma. The voice that says you have to earn trust through massive effort. But that voice is wrong.

Trust is built in the accumulation of small, consistent, reliable actions. Not in dramatic gestures.

One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery is Trauma and Recovery (paid link). Judith Herman's work lays out exactly how trauma destroys trust and what the rebuilding process actually looks like. It's not quick. It's not pretty. But it's honest.

Your body knows before your mind does

I spent years trying to think my way into self-trust. I read books. I did cognitive behavioral therapy. I made lists of evidence that contradicted my negative beliefs. And none of it worked. Because self-trust isn't a thought. It's a felt sense. It's a body experience.

Something I think about often is the concept of "good enough." Not perfect healing. Not complete resolution. Just good enough. Winnicott used this phrase for parenting - the "good enough mother." I think it applies to recovery too. Good enough recovery. Where you're not cured but you're not drowning. Where you still get triggered but you know what to do. Where you still have hard days but they don't define you.

Deb Dana calls this "glimmers" - those small moments of ventral vagal activation. A warm cup of tea. A genuine laugh. A moment of feeling connected. They're not dramatic. They're not Instagram-worthy. But they're real. And they add up. Every glimmer is a deposit in your nervous system's safety account. Every one matters.

Right now, can you think of one glimmer from today? One moment - even half a second - where your body felt okay? Where you weren't bracing or scanning or performing? Hold that. Let it land. Let your body register that it happened. Because your nervous system has a negativity bias - it remembers threats much more easily than safety. So you have to actively help it notice the good. Not to bypass the hard stuff. But to balance it. To remind your body that safety exists too. Even if it's just for a moment.

When you trust yourself, your body does something specific. Your shoulders drop. Your breath deepens. Your chest opens. You feel grounded. You feel present. You feel capable. And when you don't trust yourself, your body does something else. Your shoulders hike up. Your breath gets shallow. Your chest tightens. You feel floaty. Disconnected. Like you're watching yourself from a distance.

This is why body-based work is so important for rebuilding self-trust. You can't think your way into feeling safe in your own skin. You have to practice it. In your body. Over and over.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link). Deb Dana's work gives you actual tools for tracking your nervous system states and learning to trust the signals your body is sending you.

Here's a practice I use. It's simple. When I notice I'm doubting myself, I pause. I put my hand on my chest. I take three slow breaths. And I ask my body one question: "What do you need right now?" Not what my mind thinks I should do. Not what the trauma script says. Just what my body needs. Sometimes it's water. Sometimes it's a nap. Sometimes it's to cry. Sometimes it's to move. I don't judge the answer. I just receive it. And then I try to give it to myself.

That's a trust-building moment. Every single time I do that, I'm telling my nervous system, "I hear you. I see you. I'll respond to you." And slowly, my system starts to believe me.

You will fail at this. That's part of the process.

I want to be honest with you about something. You will break promises to yourself. You will fall back into self-doubt. You will have days where you don't trust yourself at all. That's not failure. That's the nonlinear reality of healing from complex trauma.

The goal is not perfect self-trust. The goal is a growing ability to return to self-trust when you lose it. To notice the rupture and repair it. To say, "Okay, I broke my promise. What do I need to do to rebuild trust with myself now?" Not shame. Not punishment. Repair.

This is where a lot of people get stuck. They think self-trust is a destination you arrive at. But it's not. It's a practice. A daily, sometimes hourly, practice. Some days it flows easily. Other days it feels impossible. Both are part of the process.

A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical is The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link). It gives you concrete steps for rebuilding trust with yourself, even when it feels like you're starting from zero.

Here's what I know for sure. The return of self-trust is not a luxury. It's not something you do after you've healed the "real" issues. It is the foundation. Everything else. Every boundary. Every relationship. Every decision. Every moment of joy. Every moment of rest. All of it rests on this one thing.

Can you trust yourself to take the next small step? Not the big one. Not the perfect one. Just the next one. That's all you need.

I don't know where you are in this process. Maybe you're just realizing how little you trust yourself. Maybe you've been working on this for years. Maybe you're somewhere in between. Wherever you are, it's okay. You're not broken. You're not behind. You're exactly where you need to be.

And the fact that you're still here, still reading, still searching for a way back to yourself? That's proof that some part of you still trusts. That some part of you still believes that return is possible.

Trust that part. It knows what it's talking about.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Anchored (paid link) - A practical guide to working with your nervous system
  • Radical Acceptance (paid link) - A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection
  • Trauma and Recovery (paid link) - One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery
  • The Complex PTSD Workbook (paid link) - A structured workbook that many C-PTSD survivors find practical
